And so what we started to realize, this is kind of like the crazy part, was as we started to grow and scale, and this was right around like kind of January this year.
We started to realize that all these tools to build workflows, they were really flawed because they were all trying to abstract the way code, right?
And so if you use any of these automation tools like NAN, Gumloop, and all of those, like, you're essentially having to prompt everything.
And then every time we would try to change something, version control was horrible, things were breaking, it was getting really slow, it getting really expensive.
And so we kind of went back to the basics.
And we already knew how to build the runtime infrastructure, because that's what he had already built at IFTTT.
So then on top of that, we essentially built an AI coding agent to represent the workflows in code, essentially.
But the coding agent allows you to essentially ask experts to interact with it.
And so because of that, we essentially built this framework to build, and I can show you here in a little bit, but workflows at scale and run them, right?
And so from there, the whole idea is like, we're delivering the services, but we're delivering the service as a way to R&D.
Our way into the workflows to do really complex tasks really, really well.
And the way to do that, the byproduct of that is we built all the infrastructure to run this at scale and now to learn from it.
And now the next layer is the task orchestration layer, essentially.
Think of it as like a task hub.
So right now it's dedicated teams on the service side, but we're starting to build like the task hub.

So that instead of having a team do a project or be dedicated to a client, that you can have a person do a certain task that is perfectly matched to that person within like multiple workflows and things, right?
Like, so all of that, like, is underneath the hood that no one sees.
